title: Multi-tenant: church subdomains (dentonbible.howwas.church)
description:
Give each church a subdomain where their staff can upload sermons and give feedback. First customer: Denton Bible Church.

## Context

DBC wants to upload sermons, view results, and give feedback. As we iterate on their needs, changes may be DBC-specific (UI, features, scoring tweaks) and we dont want to break the public site.

## Decision: Feature flags + subdomain (NOT git branches)

Git branch approach (e.g. `dbc` branch) sounds simple but is a trap for a small team. Cherry-picking fixes between branches within a week, merge drift gets painful fast. One codebase, one deployment, one branch is the way.

DBC-specific behavior is toggled by tenant context. When a feature is ready for everyone, flip the flag. No merge required.

## Architecture: Hybrid approach — shared infra, tenant-isolated data

Single SWA + single Function App. Tenant isolation via churchId filtering in Cosmos. Per-tenant config drives UI/feature divergence.

## Implementation layers

### Layer 1: DNS + routing (~30 min)
- Add wildcard CNAME: `*.howwas.church` → SWA (gentle-ground-0713e880f.1.azurestaticapps.net)
- SWA already handles custom domains; wildcard means any subdomain resolves
- Porkbun API for DNS (keys in .env, API access enabled per sermon-2g2)

### Layer 2: Tenant resolution in frontend (~1 hour)
- `useTenant()` hook reads `window.location.hostname`
- `dentonbible.howwas.church` → tenant = `denton-bible`
- `howwas.church` → tenant = null (public site, no filtering)
- Passes tenant as header (`x-tenant: denton-bible`) on every API call
- Tenant context provided via React context so all components can access it

### Layer 3: Backend data filtering (~1 hour)
- `GET /api/sermons` with `x-tenant` header → adds `WHERE c.churchId = @churchId` to Cosmos query
- `POST /api/sermons` with `x-tenant` header → auto-sets `churchId` on the new sermon doc
- No header = no filter (public site works exactly as today, backward compatible)
- Same pattern for any other list endpoints (feeds, etc.)

### Layer 4: Tenant config for UI divergence (~1 hour)
- Tenant config stored in the existing `churches` Cosmos container (add a `features` and `branding` field)
- Example: `{ "id": "denton-bible", "name": "Denton Bible", "features": { "cbvTab": true, "feedbackForm": true }, "branding": { "logo": null, "accent": null } }`
- Frontend fetches tenant config once on load via `GET /api/churches/{churchId}`
- Uses config to show/hide features: `if (tenant.features.newThing)`
- New DBC-specific feature? Add a flag, build it behind the check, deploy once — only shows on their subdomain

### Layer 5: Auth scoping (NOT day 1)
- SWA has built-in auth (AAD, GitHub). Can scope roles per tenant later.
- Day 1: DBC gets an unauthed subdomain. They are the only ones who know the URL.
- When auth is needed, add SWA role assignments per tenant.

## What we do NOT build
- Self-service signup
- Per-tenant billing
- Separate Azure resources per tenant
- Admin panel for managing tenants
- Complex RBAC
- Git branch per church

## Onboarding a new church (manual, ~5 min)
1. Create/update church doc in Cosmos with features + branding fields
2. Add DNS record (CNAME or wildcard already covers it)
3. Hand them the URL

## Existing data model support
- `churchId` already exists on every sermon document (set by ensure_church activity)
- `churches` Cosmos container already exists with church docs
- Just need to add `features` and `branding` fields to church docs

## Total effort: ~half day

## Sequencing
1. Layer 1 (DNS wildcard) — unblocks everything
2. Layer 2 (frontend tenant hook) + Layer 3 (backend filtering) — together, gives DBC a working filtered view
3. Layer 4 (tenant config) — enables UI divergence without code branches
4. Layer 5 (auth) — when DBC asks for it

## Alternatives considered

### Git branches per church
- Rejected. Merge drift, cherry-pick hell, two deployments to maintain. Doesnt scale even to 2 churches.

### Option B: Query param filtering only (e.g. ?church=denton-bible)
- Considered as quick win. Handles data isolation but not UI divergence. Would need to be replaced by subdomain approach anyway once UI changes are needed. Skip the intermediate step.

### Separate Azure resources per tenant
- Overkill. Shared infra with data filtering is sufficient for first 5-10 churches. Revisit if tenant count or isolation requirements grow.

## Cost impact
- Zero additional Azure cost (wildcard DNS, same SWA free tier, same Function App, same Cosmos)
- Per-sermon cost unchanged (~$0.75)

## Related beads
- sermon-2g2: DNS setup research (Porkbun API, SWA custom domains)
- sermon-1jo: howwas.church DNS configuration
- sermon-4c3: Multi-environment deploy.sh (closed, merged into deploy hardening)
close_reason: Layers 1-3 complete: DNS (dentonbible.howwas.church), frontend tenant resolution + x-tenant header, backend churchId filtering. Layer 4 (feature flags/branding config) deferred until DBC requests UI divergence.

title: RSS podcast feed subscription — auto-score new episodes
description: Allow users to subscribe to a podcast RSS feed URL. System polls for new episodes, downloads audio, and automatically runs the PSR pipeline. Enables passive monitoring of pastors/churches without manual uploads. Discovery: DBC Morning Messages feed (dentonbible.org/mediafiles/denton-bible-morning-messages.xml) has 274+ episodes with direct MP3 enclosure URLs — standard podcast RSS makes this straightforward.
close_reason: Already implemented — feed subscription with auto-polling (12h timer), manual poll with preview/confirm, and automatic PSR pipeline scoring is live.

title: No rate limiting on upload endpoint — concurrent uploads can exhaust LLM quota
description:
POST /api/sermons has no rate limiting. A user (or bot) could upload 100 sermons simultaneously, each kicking off 5+ parallel LLM passes. With S0 Standard tier at 50-80K TPM, even 3-4 concurrent sermons could trigger cascading 429 errors.

The Durable Functions retry policy (RETRY_LLM: 4 attempts, 60s backoff) helps, but multiple orchestrators all retrying at once makes the situation worse.

For MVP: Add a simple concurrency check — query Cosmos for sermons with status='processing', reject new uploads if count exceeds a threshold (e.g., 3). Phase 1: proper queue-based throttling.

Ref: api/function_app.py:39-90, api/host.json (no concurrency config)
close_reason: Merged into sermon-4f6 (Protect upload endpoint — auth, rate limiting, concurrency). Concurrency check and LLM quota details preserved.

title: Cleanup: gitignore POC artifacts and remove stale root files
description:
## Problem

### POC directory (412MB)
The `poc/` directory contains 20 JSON result/cache files (2.3MB) and large transcript caches that bloat the repo. These are historical research artifacts — useful for reference but shouldn't be in every clone.

Files to gitignore:
- `poc/transcript_cache*.json` (5 files, 2.3MB total — largest artifacts)
- `poc/*_result*.json` (result snapshots, useful but static)
- `poc/samples/` (sample audio files if any)

Keep tracked:
- `poc/*.py` (POC scripts — useful reference)
- `poc/README.md`

### Stale root files
- `gpt41_deploy.log` (36KB) — one-time deploy log, no ongoing value
- `retry_gpt41_deploy.sh` (623B) — one-time retry script, no ongoing value

### .env in repo
- `.env` is tracked (702B). Only `.env.example` should be tracked. Add `.env` to `.gitignore` and remove from tracking with `git rm --cached .env`.

## Plan

1. Add to `.gitignore`: `poc/*.json`, `poc/samples/`, `.env`
2. `git rm --cached` the JSON files, .env, and stale root files
3. Commit: removes from tracking but keeps local copies
4. Verify repo clone size drops significantly
close_reason: Only one file was still tracked (poc/samples/bad_sermon_script.txt). The .gitignore already had all the right rules — .env, poc/*.json, poc/samples/, gpt41_deploy.log, retry_gpt41_deploy.sh were all previously cleaned up. Untracked the last straggler.

title: Refinement: move radar chart above category cards for screenshot-friendly fold
description:
## Problem

Design principle #5 says the top fold (score + summary + radar) should look good shared on Twitter/Discord. Currently the layout order in SermonDetail.tsx is: score gauge → summary → 8 category cards (2×4 grid) → radar chart. The category cards push the radar well below the fold on most viewports.

## Research

### Current layout (web/src/components/SermonDetail.tsx)
The render order in the "complete" status block:
1. Score gauge (ScoreGauge component) — ~250px height
2. Summary text (italic, centered) — ~40px
3. Category cards (2×4 grid, `mt-10`) — ~600px on desktop, ~1200px on mobile (stacked)
4. Radar chart (RadarView, `mt-10`) — 400×400px
5. Strengths/Improvements (2-col grid, `mt-10`)
6. Transcript viewer (`mt-10`)

On a 1080p screen, the fold is roughly at ~700px. Score gauge + summary fits (~300px). The 8 category cards take ~600px. Radar starts at ~900px — well below the fold.

On mobile (375px width), cards stack to single column: 8 cards × ~120px = ~960px. Radar starts at ~1260px.

### Screenshot-friendly fold target
For Twitter/Discord sharing, the ideal screenshot captures: big score number + summary + radar shape. This tells the whole story in one image. Currently you'd need to scroll past all 8 category cards to see the radar.

### Fix
Move the `<RadarView>` component directly after the summary, before the category cards grid. The new order:
1. Score gauge
2. Summary
3. Radar chart ← moved up
4. Category cards
5. Strengths/Improvements
6. Transcript

This puts score + summary + radar in ~700px — fits in one fold on desktop, and within 1.5 scrolls on mobile.

### Alternative considered
Collapse category cards by default (only show on click). Rejected — the cards are the main content; hiding them hurts usability.

### Files to change
- `web/src/components/SermonDetail.tsx`: swap the order of the RadarView and category cards grid sections
close_reason: Swapped RadarView above category cards grid in SermonDetail.tsx. Score gauge + summary + radar now fit in ~700px — one fold on desktop. Deployed.

title: Security & code review hardening
description: Full code review findings from 2026-04-03. 17 items across CRITICAL (3), HIGH (3), MEDIUM (6), LOW (5). See issue body for details. Most critical: _require_admin accepts forged x-ms-client-principal headers — confirmed exploitable (deleted a sermon during testing, re-ingested via feed poll). Function App is directly accessible, bypassing SWA auth entirely.
close_reason: Critical items resolved: forged x-ms-client-principal exploit fixed (_require_admin checks admin role only), EasyAuth declared in Bicep, SWA linked backend active. Full 17-item list from original review not preserved in bead — create new issues if specific remaining items surface.

title: Refactor: split function_app.py into route modules
description:
## Problem

function_app.py is 1,972 lines — every HTTP endpoint, helper, and orchestrator in one file. Agents must read 100+ lines of context to find insertion points. Hard to navigate, easy to create merge conflicts.

## Research

Azure Functions Python v2 supports `func.Blueprint()` for HTTP routes (same pattern as `df.Blueprint()` already used for durable functions). Blueprints register on the main app via `app.register_functions(bp)`.

Current route groups (18 endpoints):
- Sermons: 8 routes (upload, upload_text, upload_youtube, list, get, translate, bonus, delete, edit)
- Churches: 3 routes (list, upsert, delete)
- Feeds: 6 routes (list, create, update, delete, poll, preview)
- Admin: 1 route (rescore)
- Helpers: _require_admin, _json_response, _feeds_container, _extract_text, _extract_video_id, _parse_timestamp, _default_audio_metrics

## Plan

Split into:
- `api/function_app.py` — app init, register blueprints (~30 lines)
- `api/routes/sermons.py` — sermon CRUD + upload endpoints (~650 lines)
- `api/routes/feeds.py` — feed CRUD + poll + preview (~350 lines)
- `api/routes/churches.py` — church CRUD (~150 lines)
- `api/routes/admin.py` — rescore endpoint (~50 lines)
- `api/helpers.py` — shared helpers (_require_admin, _json_response, etc.)
- `api/orchestrators.py` — durable function orchestrators + activity bindings (already on bp)

Each route file creates its own `bp = func.Blueprint()` and exports it. function_app.py imports and registers all blueprints.

## Test impact

Existing tests import directly from function_app.py — imports will need updating to new module paths. All 130 tests must pass after refactor.

## Risk

Low — pure restructuring, no logic changes. Deploy with `./infra/deploy.sh backend` and verify all endpoints respond.
close_reason: Split function_app.py (1,972 lines) into 7 modules: helpers.py, routes/{sermons,feeds,churches,admin}.py, orchestrators.py. function_app.py is now 29 lines. All 130 tests pass, deployed and verified.

title: Throttle-aware rate limit scheduler for LLM passes
close_reason: Implemented throttle-aware scheduler in azure_multipass_poc.py and validated_multipass_poc.py. Estimates tokens per pass, groups by deployment, runs parallel only when safe. Updated research.md and sermonplan.md with learnings.

title: Verify Parselmouth builds on Azure Functions Linux Consumption (Python 3.12)
description:
Parselmouth (praat-parselmouth) is a 36MB C++ native extension. The sermonplan acknowledges it CAN run on Azure Functions Linux but this has never been tested in the actual deployment. If it fails to load at runtime, the activity_analyze_audio step silently degrades (audio_metrics=None) and delivery/emotional_range scores become text-only estimates.

The graceful degradation path works (function_app.py:178-182), but the scores will be less accurate for every sermon until this is fixed.

Test: Deploy to Azure Functions and call the analyze_audio activity directly. Check Application Insights for any import errors on parselmouth.

Ref: api/requirements.txt:4, api/activities.py:112-158, infra/modules/functions.bicep:30 (Python 3.12)
close_reason: Parselmouth confirmed working on Azure Functions Python 3.12 (4/5 audio sermons have real metrics). Fixed fallback: pass audioAvailable flag so LLM scores conservatively from transcript cues instead of interpreting zeros. Added gpt-5-mini/nano and gpt-4.1-nano infra deployments.

title: Bug: temp file suffix hardcoded to .mp3 for all audio formats
description:
activities.py lines 69 and 123: both transcribe() and analyze_audio() save downloaded audio to a temp file with hardcoded .mp3 suffix regardless of actual format. WAV and M4A files are saved as .mp3.

The Azure Fast Transcription API may use the file extension to determine codec. A WAV file saved as .mp3 could fail transcription or produce garbled results.

Fix: Extract the actual extension from the blob URL (which contains the original filename) and use it for the temp file suffix.

Ref: api/activities.py:69, api/activities.py:123
close_reason: Both temp file locations (transcribe line 69, analyze_audio line 123) now extract the actual file extension from blobUrl via os.path.splitext, falling back to .mp3 if none. WAV and M4A files are no longer misnamed.
